paragraph: Researchers at the Valdenmoor Institute for Photonic Engineering, part of Cresthaven Technical University in Solberg, Norway, have demonstrated a metalens array capable of focusing light at 532 nanometers with a numerical aperture of 1.42 and a focusing efficiency of 94.3 percent, surpassing the previous benchmark of 87.1 percent set by a competing group in 2022. The lens structure, composed of titanium dioxide nanopillars ranging in height from 220 to 480 nanometers and arranged on a fused silica substrate measuring 12 millimeters in diameter, was fabricated using electron-beam lithography at a resolution of 8 nanometers. Dr. Helga Sørensdal and Dr. Pieter Vandekroon led the six-member team, whose findings were published in the journal Optics and Nanofabrication Quarterly on February 7, 2025. The metalens achieved a diffraction-limited spot size of 195 nanometers, enabling its application in high-resolution fluorescence microscopy at frame rates exceeding 400 hertz. Thermal stability tests confirmed less than 0.3 percent focal-length drift across a temperature range of minus 20 to plus 80 degrees Celsius, making the device suitable for deployment in field-portable imaging systems intended for environmental monitoring in Arctic regions.

A team of materials scientists at the Larkwood Center for Plasmonic Research, housed within Fennvale University in Quelsburg, Austria, has engineered a gold-silver bimetallic nanostructure array that exhibits a localized surface plasmon resonance peak at 720 nanometers with a full width at half maximum of just 18 nanometers, representing a quality factor of 40 that exceeds any previously reported value for solution-processed plasmonic films. The nanostructures, which adopt a truncated-bipyramid geometry with base lengths of 95 nanometers and aspect ratios of 2.4, were synthesized through a seed-mediated colloidal growth protocol and deposited via Langmuir-Blodgett assembly onto indium tin oxide substrates. Dr. Astrid Halvormann and postdoctoral researcher Dr. Emeka Obiechina reported the work in the Plasmonic Materials and Devices journal on January 22, 2025. The array demonstrated an electromagnetic field enhancement factor of 3,800 at the nanoparticle junctions, enabling single-molecule surface-enhanced Raman scattering detection with a spectral acquisition time of 0.8 seconds per spectrum. Potential applications cited by the team include point-of-care biosensing platforms targeting protein biomarkers at concentrations as low as 0.4 femtomolar in undiluted serum.

Scientists at the Thorveld Photonics Laboratory of Kessmark University in Dubringen, Switzerland, have fabricated a three-dimensional silicon photonic crystal exhibiting a complete photonic bandgap spanning 1,430 to 1,610 nanometers, covering 118 nanometers of the telecommunications C and L bands simultaneously. The structure, built using a layer-by-layer woodpile assembly of silicon rods with a rod diameter of 120 nanometers and a lattice constant of 470 nanometers, was produced over a 2.1-centimeter-square chip area using focused-ion-beam milling followed by atomic layer deposition of a 14-nanometer hafnium oxide conformal coating. Dr. Marcellus Treuburg and Dr. Yuki Nishizawa led the fabrication effort, publishing their results in the journal Advanced Photonic Crystals on March 3, 2025. Transmission measurements confirmed a peak attenuation of 62 decibels within the bandgap, while guided modes outside the gap exhibited propagation losses of only 0.7 decibels per centimeter. Integration of a linear-defect waveguide channel within the crystal demonstrated a group velocity of 0.04 times the speed of light in vacuum, relevant to optical buffering applications in next-generation wavelength-division-multiplexed data networks.

Engineers at the Mirefall Institute of Fiber and Guided-Wave Technologies, affiliated with Westbronn Polytechnic in Calderhaven, Wales, have developed an ultralow-loss hollow-core photonic bandgap fiber that transmits light at 1,550 nanometers with an attenuation coefficient of 0.11 decibels per kilometer, the lowest value ever recorded for a hollow-core design and competitive with the best commercially available solid-core fibers. The fiber, designated HCF-Δ9, features a 26-micrometers-diameter hollow core surrounded by seven rings of silica capillaries with wall thicknesses of 430 nanometers, yielding a bandwidth of 320 nanometers centered on the C-band. Dr. Callum Ashbridge and Dr. Priya Sunderarajan detailed the fiber's properties in a report published in the Fiber Optics and Guided Lightwave journal on April 15, 2025. Dispersion measurements revealed a group-velocity dispersion of minus 2.1 picoseconds per nanometer per kilometer, enabling transmission of 100-gigabaud polarization-multiplexed signals over 1,200 kilometers without in-line optical amplification. Nonlinear phase accumulation was found to be 98 times lower than in equivalent solid-core fibers, opening pathways for high-fidelity quantum key distribution links between metropolitan centers.

Physicists at the Quandrel Attosecond Science Center, a division of Helmark University in Rijnstad, the Netherlands, have generated isolated attosecond pulses with a duration of 43 attoseconds and a peak photon energy of 95 electron-volts using a two-color driving laser scheme combining an 800-nanometer, 25-femtosecond fundamental pulse with its second harmonic at 400 nanometers. The two beams, with an intensity ratio of 3.7 to 1 and a relative delay precision of 0.6 attoseconds, drove high-harmonic generation in a krypton gas jet at a backing pressure of 180 millibars, producing a photon flux of 2.1 times ten to the eleventh photons per second at the harmonic orders spanning H55 through H73. Dr. Liesbeth Verbruggen and Dr. Andrei Volkonsky led the experimental campaign, reported in Ultrafast Photonics Letters on May 2, 2025. The resulting attosecond pulse train was characterized via attosecond streaking with a residual timing jitter below 80 attoseconds, and was subsequently applied to track inner-shell electron dynamics in xenon with a temporal resolution of 60 attoseconds. The work establishes a new benchmark for table-top extreme-ultraviolet sources operating below the 50-attosecond barrier.

A collaborative research group at the Ostrand Quantum Dot Facility, jointly operated by Fenwick-Saarlow University and the Greyholm Academy of Materials in Brentwick, Canada, has synthesized lead-free cesium copper iodide quantum dots with a photoluminescence quantum yield of 96.8 percent at an emission wavelength of 445 nanometers and a full width at half maximum of 12 nanometers. The nanocrystals, with an average diameter of 5.2 nanometers and a size dispersity below 7 percent, were produced via a room-temperature ligand-assisted reprecipitation method using oleylamine and oleic acid capping agents in a dimethylformamide solvent system. Dr. Saoirse MacGillivray and Dr. Lorenzo Fabretti published the synthesis protocol in the journal Colloidal Semiconductor Photonics on December 10, 2024. Time-resolved photoluminescence measurements revealed a biexponential decay with lifetimes of 3.8 and 18.4 nanoseconds, attributed to exciton and multi-exciton recombination channels respectively. When integrated into a down-conversion film on a 450-nanometer InGaN LED, the quantum dots produced white-light emission with a correlated color temperature of 4,100 kelvin and a color rendering index of 97, demonstrating viability for high-quality solid-state lighting without cadmium or lead compounds.

Researchers at the Carndell Institute for Nonlinear Optics, part of Ashvale University in Stonehurst, Scotland, have demonstrated sum-frequency generation in a periodically poled lithium tantalate waveguide with a conversion efficiency of 38 percent per watt-squared per centimeter-squared, achieved at a pump wavelength of 1,064 nanometers and a signal wavelength of 1,540 nanometers producing output at 637 nanometers. The waveguide, measuring 4.2 centimeters in length with a cross-sectional dimension of 3.0 by 1.8 micrometers, was fabricated using proton exchange followed by reverse proton exchange annealing to produce a low-loss channel with propagation losses of 0.4 decibels per centimeter. Dr. Fionn Dalgety and Dr. Miriam Szablewski reported the results in Nonlinear Photonics and Crystal Optics on November 18, 2024. The device operated at room temperature without active temperature stabilization, maintaining conversion efficiency within 1.2 percent over a 15-degree Celsius ambient temperature swing, owing to the quasi-phase-matching period of 8.7 micrometers selected to exploit a natural thermal compensation crossing point. Applications in quantum frequency conversion for fiber-to-atom qubit interfaces were highlighted by the authors as the primary near-term use case.

A research group led by Dr. Ingvar Torblansen at the Nordfjeld Laser Physics Institute of Stormveil University in Fjordvik, Iceland, has realized continuous-wave lasing at 2,790 nanometers from a holmium-doped yttrium aluminum garnet crystal pumped by a 1,940-nanometer thulium fiber laser delivering 8.5 watts of incident power. The crystal, measuring 5 by 5 by 20 millimeters with a holmium doping concentration of 0.8 atomic percent, was mounted in a hemispherical resonator with mirror reflectivities of 99.8 and 70 percent at the lasing wavelength, achieving a slope efficiency of 62 percent and a maximum output power of 4.3 watts. Results were detailed in the journal Mid-Infrared Laser Engineering on October 5, 2024, with co-authors Dr. Solveig Hrafnkelsdottir and Dr. Dmitri Belyayev. Beam quality measurements yielded an M-squared value of 1.07, confirming near-diffraction-limited output compatible with precision infrared materials processing and medical tissue ablation at the strong water absorption band near 2,800 nanometers. Mode-hop-free single-frequency operation over a 480-megahertz tuning range was demonstrated by applying a piezoelectric end-mirror actuator with a bandwidth of 18 kilohertz.

Scientists at the Brackmoor Advanced Metamaterials Laboratory, a joint venture between Crandall University and the Oswick Institute of Technology in Aldensham, England, have designed and characterized a hyperbolic metamaterial stack composed of alternating layers of silver and magnesium fluoride, each pair having a period of 32 nanometers, that supports negative refraction at 488 nanometers with a real part of the effective refractive index of minus 1.8. The 48-layer stack, deposited by magnetron sputtering on a sapphire substrate with individual thickness tolerances below 0.4 nanometers, exhibited a broadband negative-refraction window spanning 470 to 540 nanometers and an imaginary part of the refractive index below 0.3 across this range. Dr. Wallace Pemberton and Dr. Claudine Desroches published the optical characterization data in Hyperbolic Photonics Annals on September 27, 2024. Prism-coupling experiments confirmed a refraction angle of minus 24 degrees for a collimated beam incident at 45 degrees, consistent with transfer-matrix simulations. The team demonstrated subwavelength imaging of a 180-nanometer chrome grating through an 800-nanometer-thick slab of the metamaterial, resolving features at a spatial frequency of 2.6 times the free-space wave vector and establishing a new performance threshold for far-field superlenses in the visible spectrum.

Engineers at the Pelworth Terahertz Systems Group of Ravensbrook University in Edenmoor, Australia, have constructed a broadband terahertz time-domain spectrometer utilizing a biased indium gallium arsenide photoconductive antenna driven by a 90-femtosecond, 1,030-nanometer erbium fiber laser with a repetition rate of 100 megahertz and an average power of 320 milliwatts. The system achieves a usable bandwidth of 0.1 to 7.8 terahertz with a dynamic range exceeding 85 decibels at 1.0 terahertz and a spectral resolution of 6.2 gigahertz, determined by the 160-picosecond acquisition window. Dr. Renata Kowalczyk and Dr. Alistair Dunmore described the spectrometer in Terahertz Science and Technology Reports on August 14, 2024. Time-domain waveforms were acquired in 2.4 seconds using asynchronous optical sampling, eliminating mechanical delay-line components and reducing system vibration sensitivity by a factor of 220. Initial applications demonstrated refractive index mapping of pharmaceutical tablet coatings at 1.5 terahertz with a spatial resolution of 310 micrometers, revealing coating thickness non-uniformities of 3 to 18 micrometers that correlated with dissolution rate variations of up to 14 percent in controlled release formulations.

A team led by Dr. Anastasia Veltmann at the Groenburg Center for Integrated Photonics, affiliated with Veldtmeer University in Harenstein, Germany, has fabricated a silicon nitride photonic integrated circuit embedding 24 microring resonators on a single 25-by-25-millimeter chip, achieving a chip-scale optical frequency comb spanning 400 nanometers centered at 1,560 nanometers with a repetition rate of 98.7 gigahertz and a phase noise floor of minus 145 decibels per hertz at a 1-megahertz offset. Each ring resonator has a radius of 100 micrometers, a loaded quality factor of 1.4 million, and a cross-coupled power splitting ratio tunable via integrated thermo-optic phase shifters operating at 14 milliwatts per pi phase shift. The results were published in Integrated Photonic Circuits and Systems on July 22, 2024. The comb was used to perform dual-comb spectroscopy of carbon dioxide absorption lines between 1,570 and 1,610 nanometers with a frequency accuracy of 180 kilohertz, achieving a sensitivity of 2.4 parts per billion per meter of optical path length. The team further demonstrated coherent wavelength-division-multiplexed data transmission at 400 gigabits per second across eight channels using the comb lines as coherent carriers, with a bit-error rate below 10 to the minus 12.

Researchers at the Delphmar Institute of Optical Materials, part of Coldwell Technological University in Selhaven, Denmark, have synthesized a perovskite-based electroluminescent device exhibiting an external quantum efficiency of 28.4 percent at a current density of 20 milliamperes per square centimeter and an emission peak at 518 nanometers with a full width at half maximum of 17 nanometers. The green-emitting device uses a formamidinium lead bromide active layer 36 nanometers thick, sandwiched between a zinc oxide electron transport layer and a poly(9-vinylcarbazole) hole transport layer, with a total device stack height of 185 nanometers deposited on indium tin oxide-coated glass. Dr. Søren Lindegaard-Poulsen and Dr. Jiyeon Hwang described the fabrication and characterization in Perovskite Optoelectronics on June 18, 2024. Operational lifetime tests at 100 candelas per square meter of initial luminance revealed a T50 half-life of 3,800 hours under continuous current driving, representing a fourfold improvement over prior reports at equivalent efficiency. The color gamut coverage reached 148 percent of the sRGB standard when combined with red and blue co-emitters, suggesting utility in ultra-high-definition display backlighting systems.

Physicists at the Blaustein Holography and Diffractive Optics Unit, housed within Waldenfels University in Breckburg, Belgium, have recorded a full-color reflection hologram using a pulsed neodymium-doped yttrium vanadate laser operating simultaneously at 1,064, 532, and 355 nanometers with pulse durations of 7 nanoseconds and a peak power of 6 megawatts, enabling single-shot exposure of a 200-by-200-millimeter recording plate. The photosensitive medium, a panchromatic silver halide emulsion designated PH-7X with a grain size below 8 nanometers, was developed with a pyrogallic acid-based developer to yield diffraction efficiencies of 31, 38, and 29 percent at the red, green, and blue Bragg wavelengths respectively. Dr. Pieter-Jan Maeseele and Dr. Sabrina Ludecke reported the work in Holographic Imaging and Display on May 10, 2024. The reconstructed image exhibited a luminance of 420 candelas per square meter under 800-lux white-light illumination and a color gamut covering 91 percent of the DCI-P3 standard. Viewing-angle bandwidth was measured at 42 degrees in the horizontal and 28 degrees in the vertical, achieved by tuning the emulsion swelling ratio to 1.06 during processing.

Engineers at the Stormfield Semiconductor Laser Group of Brennecke University in Haselburg, Germany, have developed a gallium nitride vertical-cavity surface-emitting laser emitting at 405 nanometers with a threshold current density of 3.8 kiloamperes per square centimeter and a maximum continuous-wave output power of 18.5 milliwatts from a 6-micrometer-diameter aperture, representing a wallplug efficiency of 34 percent at the peak operating point. The device features a 12-pair aluminum gallium nitride / gallium nitride distributed Bragg reflector with a stopband centered at 405 nanometers and a peak reflectivity of 99.6 percent, etched by inductively coupled plasma with a surface roughness below 0.5 nanometers root mean square. Dr. Klaus Pfeifersberg and Dr. Mi-Young Kang published the device design and performance data in Semiconductor Laser Photonics on April 2, 2024. Coherent beam characterization yielded an M-squared value of 1.12 and a beam divergence full angle of 4.8 degrees. Single-mode operation was confirmed across the full operating current range from 12 to 45 milliamperes, with a side-mode suppression ratio of 44 decibels, enabling application in holographic data storage systems requiring spatial coherence lengths exceeding 1 meter.

A multinational team coordinated by the Arventhal Consortium for Photovoltaic Photonics, involving researchers from Drekenberg University in Ulstrom, Sweden, and the Caldorff Materials Institute in Montbretonne, France, has demonstrated a photonic light-trapping structure that increases the short-circuit current density of a 120-nanometer-thick amorphous silicon solar cell by 62 percent compared to a planar reference, reaching 18.3 milliamperes per square centimeter under AM1.5G illumination at 100 milliwatts per square centimeter. The structure consists of hexagonally arranged silicon dioxide nanocones with a height of 210 nanometers, a base diameter of 160 nanometers, and a pitch of 280 nanometers, conformally coated with 40 nanometers of silver on their outer surfaces to introduce plasmonic scattering at wavelengths of 550 to 750 nanometers. Drs. Annelore Bergstrom and Thierry Malenfant led the analysis, publishing in Photovoltaic Science and Engineering on March 11, 2024. Optical absorption measurements showed 94 percent broadband absorption between 350 and 750 nanometers, compared to 62 percent for a planar cell with an anti-reflection coating, while carrier collection efficiency remained above 88 percent throughout the absorber volume, confirmed by electron beam-induced current mapping with 15-nanometer spatial resolution.

Scientists at the Harvelbrook Optical Coatings Laboratory, affiliated with Trentfield University in Calloway, Canada, have deposited an ion-beam sputtered silicon dioxide and hafnium dioxide multilayer mirror comprising 34 alternating quarter-wave layers on a 150-millimeter fused silica substrate, achieving a reflectivity of 99.9985 percent at 1,064 nanometers with an absorption loss below 0.4 parts per million and a scatter loss of 0.9 parts per million. The mirror, designed as a high-finesse cavity end mirror for gravitational-wave detector prototypes, sustains a laser-induced damage threshold of 52 joules per square centimeter under 10-nanosecond pulsed illumination at a 10-hertz repetition rate. Dr. Harriet Caldwell-Fitch and Dr. Yasushi Ozaki reported the coating performance in Precision Optical Coatings and Metrology on February 5, 2024. Cavity finesse measurements using a triangular ring cavity yielded a value of 1.4 million, corresponding to a photon round-trip lifetime of 4.6 microseconds. Coating uniformity across the 100-millimeter clear aperture was better than 0.02 percent in optical thickness, verified by scanning interferometric mapping at a spatial sampling density of 1 point per square millimeter.

Investigators at the Novalend Institute for Photonic Sensing, a research division of Brackstone University in Siltmore, New Zealand, have demonstrated a distributed fiber-optic strain sensor based on Brillouin optical time-domain analysis that achieves a spatial resolution of 4 centimeters over a 10-kilometer sensing fiber while maintaining a strain accuracy of 2 microstrain and a temperature accuracy of 0.12 degrees Celsius. The measurement employs differential pulse-width pair coding with pulse durations of 10 and 30 nanoseconds at a carrier wavelength of 1,550 nanometers, with a Brillouin frequency shift sensitivity of 48 megahertz per millistrain. Dr. Tamsin Whitmore and Dr. Hyeong-Jin Lim published the system architecture in Distributed Fiber Optic Sensing on January 8, 2024. The sensor, embedded in a 10-kilometer carbon fiber composite panel used in aerospace fuselage testing, detected a localized delamination event of 4.5 square centimeters at a location 6.34 kilometers from the interrogation unit, with a false-alarm probability below 0.2 percent over a 72-hour continuous monitoring period. Data acquisition was performed at a repetition rate of 5 kilohertz, enabling real-time structural health monitoring during cyclic fatigue load testing at 8 hertz.

A research team at the Ovengard Nonlinear Photonics Center of Kleffbruck University in Merschheim, Germany, has observed optical parametric oscillation in a chip-scale aluminum nitride microring resonator at a pump power threshold of 8.2 milliwatts, generating signal and idler beams at 1,467 and 1,651 nanometers from a 1,550-nanometer pump with a conversion efficiency of 12.4 percent in the undepleted-pump regime. The resonator, with a ring radius of 60 micrometers and a waveguide cross-section of 2.0 by 0.8 micrometers, exhibited a free spectral range of 376 gigahertz and an intrinsic quality factor of 870,000 at 1,550 nanometers before coupling. Dr. Gertrude Klausmeyer and Dr. Fabian Strothmeier reported the results in Chip-Scale Nonlinear Photonics on December 17, 2023. The aluminum nitride platform was selected for its large second-order nonlinear coefficient of 4.9 picometers per volt and its transparency window spanning 210 nanometers to 6.2 micrometers, enabling parametric down-conversion without two-photon absorption at telecommunications wavelengths. Photon-pair generation rates at threshold pump power reached 1.8 million pairs per second per milliwatt-squared, indicating applicability to entangled photon sources for quantum communication protocols.

Researchers at the Valcross Photonics Research Establishment, a facility of Dunmaren University in Strath Eldin, Scotland, have designed a metasurface beam-steering device operating at 1,550 nanometers that achieves a full steering range of 80 degrees by 80 degrees in two dimensions with a steering speed of 18 microseconds per step and a far-field beam divergence of 0.06 degrees. The metasurface, measuring 8 by 8 millimeters and containing 640,000 phase-shifting unit cells each incorporating a liquid-crystal-tunable silicon Mie resonator, achieves a 0-to-2-pi phase modulation range at an actuation voltage of 6.5 volts root mean square with a modulation bandwidth of 85 kilohertz. Dr. Catriona Dunbar-Selkirk and Dr. Nikolaos Papadimitriou published the beam-steering results in Adaptive Photonic Metasurfaces on November 4, 2023. Far-field intensity measurements confirmed side-lobe suppression of 18 decibels across the full steering range, compatible with lidar point-cloud acquisition at a range of 150 meters and a lateral resolution of 16 centimeters. The device consumes 42 milliwatts of peak electrical power, and its compatibility with standard complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor driver circuitry was validated using a foundry 180-nanometer process node at Eldin Semiconductor Foundry.

Scientists at the Froeberg Ultrafast Spectroscopy Division of Kjelland University in Stavnord, Norway, have applied two-dimensional electronic spectroscopy at 17,000-wavenumber central frequency to resolve energy-transfer pathways in a synthetic porphyrin J-aggregate with a coherence time of 320 femtoseconds at room temperature, identifying four distinct excitonic states separated by 180, 430, and 620 wavenumbers. The laser system used for the experiment produces 12-femtosecond pulses with a bandwidth of 2,600 wavenumbers centered at 590 nanometers at a repetition rate of 250 kilohertz and a pulse energy of 4.2 nanojoules. Dr. Ingrid Haugenbakken and Dr. Pablo Castañeda-Ruiz described the spectroscopic measurements in Ultrafast Molecular Photonics on October 21, 2023. Population transfer between the highest and lowest excitonic states was found to occur via two sequential incoherent hops with characteristic times of 85 and 210 femtoseconds, inconsistent with purely Forster-type energy transfer and suggesting contributions from vibronically coupled pathways. The aggregate's absorption cross-section at the J-band maximum of 596 nanometers was measured at 3.1 times ten to the minus 14 square centimeters, facilitating single-aggregate detection using a 0.4-milliwatt probe beam and a silicon avalanche photodiode with a dark count rate of 22 counts per second.

Engineers at the Calloden Optical Fiber Sensor Group at Redmarch University in Norwick, England, have developed a femtosecond laser-inscribed fiber Bragg grating array in a 125-micrometer-diameter sapphire optical fiber designed to operate at temperatures up to 1,900 degrees Celsius, the highest reported for any grating-based point sensor. The gratings, inscribed using a 520-nanometer, 350-femtosecond laser at a pulse energy of 80 nanojoules and a writing speed of 0.5 millimeters per second, produce a reflection peak at 847 nanometers with a reflectivity of 18 percent and a bandwidth of 0.4 nanometers at room temperature. Dr. Malcolm Ashcroft and Dr. Rosamund Partington published the sensor performance in High-Temperature Fiber Photonics on September 9, 2023. Calibration data collected between 500 and 1,850 degrees Celsius showed a Bragg wavelength shift coefficient of 22 picometers per degree Celsius, with linearity of 99.4 percent and a hysteresis below 0.8 degrees Celsius after three thermal cycles. Embedded in a molybdenum sheath and deployed in a test turbine combustor burning methane at an equivalence ratio of 0.85, the sensor withstood 480 hours of continuous operation at 1,740 degrees Celsius, confirming stability for gas turbine health monitoring applications.

A team at the Dresslar Center for Nanophotonic Devices, part of Holmgaard University in Fjelsted, Denmark, has realized a single-photon emitter based on a hexagonal boron nitride flake hosting a vacancy defect designated hBN-V3, exhibiting a zero-phonon line at 582 nanometers with a linewidth of 0.8 nanometers at 4 kelvin and a lifetime-limited coherence time of 1.4 nanoseconds. The defect was created by irradiation with a 30-kiloelectron-volt helium ion beam at a fluence of 5 times ten to the twelfth ions per square centimeter, followed by annealing at 850 degrees Celsius in a nitrogen atmosphere for 2 hours. Dr. Bjorn Kjeldsgaard and Dr. Fatima Al-Rashidi published the emitter characterization in Solid-State Quantum Photonics on August 3, 2023. Second-order photon correlation measurements yielded a g-squared-of-zero value of 0.04, confirming near-ideal single-photon purity, while photon count rates reached 820,000 per second under 532-nanometer continuous-wave excitation at a saturation power of 420 microwatts. Coupling to a 400-nanometer-wide silicon nitride waveguide positioned 35 nanometers below the hBN flake achieved a coupling efficiency of 78 percent, enabling on-chip quantum photonic circuit integration.

Investigators at the Klentsburg Photoacoustic Imaging Laboratory, affiliated with Westmaren University in Torfeld, Germany, have built a high-frame-rate photoacoustic microscopy system using a 532-nanometer nanosecond laser operating at a repetition rate of 30 kilohertz and a pulse energy of 40 nanojoules focused to a 2.1-micrometer lateral spot, achieving a maximum imaging speed of 1,200 B-scans per second at an axial resolution of 14 micrometers in soft tissue. Detection is performed with a 50-megahertz bandwidth ultrasound transducer array with 128 elements fabricated from polyvinylidene fluoride films and arranged in a linear aperture of 18 millimeters. Dr. Heidrun Ziegenfuss and Dr. Tomás Figueroa-Vega described the system and its performance in Biomedical Photoacoustic Systems on July 6, 2023. In vivo imaging of the chorioallantoic membrane of fertilized quail eggs revealed vascular networks at depths of 0.8 millimeters with a signal-to-noise ratio of 38 decibels, and flow velocities in vessels of 40-micrometer diameter were measured by Doppler photoacoustics at a precision of 1.4 millimeters per second. The system's combined optical and acoustic footprint occupied a 200-by-120-millimeter optics bench area, indicating feasibility for integration into endoscopic probe designs with a 6-millimeter outer diameter.

Researchers at the Thurlow Photonics and Nanofabrication Group at Kellside University in Brackport, Australia, have demonstrated a silicon-on-insulator electro-optic modulator achieving a 3-decibel electro-optic bandwidth of 112 gigahertz and an extinction ratio of 12 decibels at 1,550 nanometers, driven by a 1-volt peak-to-peak signal applied across a 4-millimeter-long Mach-Zehnder modulator arm incorporating a lateral p-n junction. The junction doping profile, optimized using drift-diffusion simulations, features a p-region concentration of 4.5 times ten to the seventeenth per cubic centimeter and an n-region of 5.1 times ten to the seventeenth per cubic centimeter, yielding a modulation efficiency of 1.1 volt-centimeters. Dr. Gareth Whittaker and Dr. Sunita Prabhukar reported the modulator in Silicon Photonic Devices and Integration on June 22, 2023. Insertion loss of the device was 3.4 decibels including grating coupler input and output ports, and the energy consumption per bit was determined to be 62 femtojoules at 112-gigabaud operation, among the lowest reported values for silicon electro-optic modulators in this bandwidth class. Data transmission experiments demonstrated error-free transmission of 224-gigabit-per-second four-level pulse amplitude modulation signals over 40 kilometers of standard single-mode fiber.

Scientists at the Brandvik Metasurface Holography Laboratory of Fjeldmoor Technical University in Rosvann, Norway, have created a dielectric metasurface hologram using silicon nanopillars on a fused silica substrate that reconstructs a full-color three-dimensional scene with a holographic image brightness of 280 candelas per square meter under 1,000-lux ambient LED illumination and a diffraction efficiency of 72 percent at 633 nanometers. The metasurface, measuring 30 by 30 millimeters and containing 2.8 billion unit cells with minimum feature sizes of 90 nanometers, was designed using a modified Gerchberg-Saxton phase-retrieval algorithm running on a 512-core tensor processing unit cluster for 14 hours. Dr. Sigrid Haugland and Dr. Kenji Nakashima described the design and fabrication process in Metasurface Holography and Imaging on May 18, 2023. The reconstructed image contained 14 depth planes spanning 30 to 200 millimeters from the metasurface, with a depth resolution of 12 millimeters determined by the aperture size and wavelength. Multiplexing of three holographic channels at 450, 532, and 633 nanometers was achieved by exploiting wavelength-selective resonance of three independently optimized pillar heights of 350, 490, and 620 nanometers respectively, enabling crosstalk between color channels below 2 percent.

Engineers at the Pendwick Laser Materials Processing Center, affiliated with Granston University of Technology in Eastholm, Canada, have developed an ultrafast laser texturing process that produces superhydrophobic surfaces on titanium alloy substrates using 280-femtosecond, 1,030-nanometer pulses at a fluence of 0.18 joules per square centimeter, a repetition rate of 400 kilohertz, and a scanning speed of 600 millimeters per second, achieving a static water contact angle of 162 degrees and a contact angle hysteresis of 2.1 degrees. The laser-induced periodic surface structures formed by this process have a spatial period of 810 nanometers and a modulation depth of 320 nanometers, analyzed by atomic force microscopy with a tip radius of 7 nanometers. Dr. Marguerite Tremblay-Archambault and Dr. Sven Holmqvist published the process parameters and wettability results in Laser Surface Engineering on April 4, 2023. Durability testing by Taber abrasion at 500-gram load over 2,000 cycles retained a contact angle above 150 degrees, and salt-spray corrosion resistance per ASTM B117 was extended by a factor of 3.4 compared to the untextured alloy. The texturing throughput of 14 square centimeters per minute makes the process compatible with inline production of aerospace structural components requiring anti-icing functionality.

A group at the Halcroft Infrared Photonics Laboratory of Mossdale University in Ringarve, Ireland, has demonstrated room-temperature laser action in a quantum cascade laser structure emitting at 8.6 micrometers with a threshold current density of 0.48 kiloamperes per square centimeter, a peak power of 2.4 watts at a duty cycle of 10 percent under pulsed operation, and a wall-plug efficiency of 18.3 percent at the operating point of 0.9 kiloamperes per square centimeter. The active region employs a bound-to-continuum transition design with 35 cascade stages, each comprising indium gallium arsenide well layers of 4.4 nanometers and aluminum indium arsenide barrier layers of 1.8 nanometers grown lattice-matched to an indium phosphide substrate by molecular beam epitaxy at a substrate temperature of 475 degrees Celsius. Dr. Orla Brennan-Coughlan and Dr. Mattias Forssberg reported the laser in Mid-Infrared Semiconductor Lasers on March 19, 2023. Far-field beam divergence was 30 degrees by 60 degrees full angle at half maximum, and single-mode emission was verified over the full current range by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy with a spectral resolution of 0.04 inverse centimeters. Methane sensing at a concentration of 18 parts per billion was demonstrated using tunable laser absorption spectroscopy with a white cell of 100-meter path length.

Researchers at the Greymount Photonic Crystal Fiber Laboratory, part of Albrecht-Drenner University in Saarfeldt, Germany, have fabricated a photonic crystal fiber with a hollow microstructured core of 22 micrometers diameter and 6 rings of air holes with a hole-to-pitch ratio of 0.94 and a pitch of 6.8 micrometers, enabling guidance of light at 1,064 nanometers with an attenuation of 4.1 decibels per kilometer and a nonlinear coefficient of 0.003 per watt per kilometer, 500 times lower than that of standard single-mode fiber. The fiber, drawn from a preform assembled by the stack-and-draw technique and drawn at 1,960 degrees Celsius at a tension of 80 grams, has an outer diameter of 160 micrometers and was coated with a dual-layer ultraviolet-cured acrylate to a final diameter of 250 micrometers. Dr. Elke Brandstetter and Dr. Yohan Mbeki published the guidance characteristics in Microstructured and Photonic Crystal Fibers on February 10, 2023. Supercontinuum generation experiments using 6-nanosecond pulses at 1,064 nanometers with a peak power of 40 kilowatts yielded a spectrum spanning 450 to 2,100 nanometers at minus 30 decibels from peak, demonstrating that the reduced nonlinearity can still sustain broadband generation for applications in optical coherence tomography and hyperspectral imaging when driven at megawatt-class peak powers.

A team at the Ventmark Plasmon-Enhanced Photocatalysis Group of Solhall University in Rimstead, Sweden, has shown that gold nanorod arrays with a longitudinal plasmon resonance at 640 nanometers can increase the apparent quantum yield of photocatalytic water splitting on titanium dioxide substrates from 1.4 percent under broadband visible illumination to 9.8 percent when the light source is spectrally matched to the plasmon resonance peak. The nanorods, with aspect ratios of 3.8 and lengths of 75 nanometers, were synthesized by cetrimonium-bromide-directed seeded growth and assembled into hexagonal arrays by DNA-origami templating with a center-to-center spacing of 120 nanometers. Dr. Helena Bryntesson and Dr. Adebayo Adeyinka reported the photocatalytic quantum yield measurements in Plasmonic Photocatalysis Advances on January 5, 2023. Hot-electron injection rates from the nanorod array into titanium dioxide were estimated at 3.4 times ten to the sixteenth electrons per second per square centimeter under 50-milliwatt-per-square-centimeter illumination at 640 nanometers, contributing 71 percent of the total photocatalytic activity with the remaining 29 percent attributed to near-field electromagnetic enhancement of titanium dioxide optical absorption. Hydrogen production rates of 140 micromoles per hour per milligram of catalyst were measured by gas chromatography.

Scientists at the Fenbridge Ultraviolet Laser Facility of Crostwick University in Aldsveld, the Netherlands, have demonstrated continuous-wave lasing at 266 nanometers using an intracavity frequency-quadrupled ytterbium fiber laser chain, achieving an output power of 780 milliwatts from a crystal of borate-based beta-phase barium metaborate measuring 5 by 5 by 8 millimeters with a phase-matching angle of 47.6 degrees and a walk-off angle of 3.8 degrees at 266 nanometers. The fundamental 1,064-nanometer ytterbium fiber oscillator delivers 8 watts in a single-frequency, linearly polarized beam with a linewidth of 2 kilohertz, frequency-doubled and subsequently quadrupled in two separate enhancement cavities. Dr. Katrien Vandecraen and Dr. Artur Grzywinski published the laser architecture in Ultraviolet and Vacuum Ultraviolet Laser Science on December 1, 2022. Beam quality at 266 nanometers was characterized with an M-squared of 1.09 and a beam diameter of 1.8 millimeters at the output coupler. The laser enables frequency-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy of semiconductor surfaces with a photon energy resolution of 0.4 millielectron-volts, determined by the laser linewidth, and was used to map work function variations on a polycrystalline copper film with a spatial resolution of 60 micrometers.

Investigators at the Strandwell Center for Optical Metamaterials, associated with Claremoor University in Havenport, England, have fabricated a chiral metamaterial consisting of gold gammadion resonators on an indium tin oxide substrate that exhibits a circular dichroism of 38 percent and a circular birefringence of 22 degrees per micrometer at 780 nanometers, the largest combined chiro-optical response reported for a planar structure in the near-infrared. The gammadion arms, each 120 nanometers wide, 40 nanometers thick, and 400 nanometers long, are arranged in a square lattice with a 700-nanometer period and were fabricated by electron-beam lithography and liftoff with a layer alignment accuracy of 4 nanometers. Dr. Fenella Rosborough and Dr. Vikram Chandrasekaran reported the metamaterial response in Chiral Nano-Optics and Sensing on October 28, 2022. Enantiomeric discrimination experiments using a 100-nanomolar solution of D- and L-tryptophan in aqueous buffer demonstrated a differential absorption signal of 18 millidegrees at 780 nanometers, enabling concentration quantification with a relative standard deviation of 3.4 percent across a concentration range of 10 to 500 nanomolar. Temperature stability of the chiral response was confirmed to within 0.8 percent between 10 and 45 degrees Celsius.

A research group at the Koldbjerg Micro-Optics and LIDAR Laboratory of Valdris Technical Institute in Elborg, Finland, has developed a flash lidar system using a 905-nanometer, 4-nanosecond pulsed vertical-cavity surface-emitting laser array with 1,024 elements operating at a peak array power of 220 watts, combined with a 32-by-32 single-photon avalanche diode receiver array with a timing jitter of 38 picoseconds per pixel and a dead time of 6 nanoseconds. The system achieves a range resolution of 3 centimeters, a maximum range of 280 meters under 100-kilolux solar background illumination, and a frame rate of 24 hertz with a field of view of 30 by 30 degrees using a 10-millimeter-diameter collimating lens. Dr. Aino Makinen-Korhonen and Dr. Bart Hullegie described the system design in Automotive Photonic Sensing Systems on September 12, 2022. Under controlled fog conditions at a meteorological visibility of 80 meters, the system retained range capability out to 55 meters with a false-alarm rate of 1.2 events per frame. Pixel-level histogramming was performed in real time on a field-programmable gate array at a clock rate of 500 megahertz, enabling multi-return extraction for canopy-penetrating forestry mapping at 15-centimeter point density.

Researchers at the Amberton Photonics Materials and Characterization Unit, housed at Dunfield University of Technology in Caldross, Scotland, have grown a zinc oxide nanowire array on a gallium-doped zinc oxide seed layer that functions as a waveguiding random laser exhibiting stimulated emission at 383 nanometers with a threshold fluence of 12 microjoules per square centimeter under 355-nanometer, 8-nanosecond pulsed excitation. The nanowires, with diameters of 80 to 150 nanometers and lengths of 5 to 12 micrometers, were grown by hydrothermal synthesis at 90 degrees Celsius for 6 hours in a zinc nitrate and hexamethylenetetramine solution with a concentration of 50 millimolar each. Dr. Elspeth Galloway and Dr. Kensuke Yamamoto reported the lasing characteristics in Nanowire Photonics and Optical Gain on August 5, 2022. Spectrally resolved emission revealed 15 to 22 lasing modes with a mode spacing of 0.8 nanometers, consistent with feedback from scattering paths of 4 to 6 micrometers length determined by backscattering mean free path analysis. The external quantum efficiency at twice the threshold fluence was 3.8 percent, and the emission polarization ratio was 0.61 parallel to the nanowire growth axis, enabling polarization-multiplexed optical tagging applications.

Engineers at the Nordholt Advanced Laser Diagnostics Group of Krestberg University in Steinmark, Austria, have implemented cavity-enhanced absorption spectroscopy for trace-gas detection of ammonia at 6,612 wavenumbers using a distributed-feedback interband cascade laser delivering 4 milliwatts in a single-frequency beam with a linewidth of 0.6 megahertz, achieving a detection limit of 48 parts per trillion by volume at a 1-second averaging time in a 1.4-meter-long enhancement cavity with a finesse of 14,500. The cavity mirrors, coated with hafnium dioxide and silicon dioxide, have a reflectivity of 99.978 percent at the detection wavelength and are positioned in a Herriot multipass configuration providing an effective optical path length of 5.6 kilometers. Dr. Christoph Waldhaus and Dr. Alicia Berrojo-Martinez published the spectrometer design in Cavity-Enhanced Laser Spectroscopy on July 18, 2022. Noise-equivalent absorption coefficients of 6.2 times ten to the minus 11 inverse centimeters per root hertz were achieved using frequency-modulation spectroscopy at a modulation frequency of 1 megahertz and a modulation depth of 0.8 megahertz. The instrument was deployed in a semiconductor cleanroom environment and detected ammonia outgassing from photoresist materials at concentrations of 0.8 to 12 parts per billion, enabling real-time process contamination monitoring at sub-minute temporal resolution.

A team at the Whitlock Institute for Two-Photon Polymerization and Additive Nanophotonics at Sternvale University in Locharden, Scotland, has fabricated free-standing three-dimensional photonic lattice structures with a minimum feature size of 90 nanometers using two-photon polymerization of a zirconium-doped hybrid organic-inorganic resin at an exposure wavelength of 515 nanometers with a pulse energy of 1.8 nanojoules and a pulse duration of 300 femtoseconds delivered through a 1.4-numerical-aperture oil-immersion objective. The photonic lattice, a face-centered cubic arrangement with a rod diameter of 110 nanometers and a unit cell parameter of 680 nanometers, exhibits a photonic stop band at 820 nanometers with a reflectivity of 42 percent along the Gamma-L direction. Dr. Agnes Drummond and Dr. Luca Carnevale described the printing process and optical characterization in Three-Dimensional Nanophotonic Fabrication on June 8, 2022. Calcination of the polymer scaffold at 550 degrees Celsius for 2 hours in air converted it to a mechanically rigid zirconium oxide ceramic lattice while shrinking linear dimensions by 24 percent, shifting the stop band to 624 nanometers. The ceramic lattice withstood compressive stresses of 28 megapascals without fracture, indicating potential for photonic structural color coatings in high-temperature environments.

Scientists at the Dalemark Quantum Photonics Laboratory of Solberg-Reinhardt University in Kjeldby, Denmark, have developed a photonic integrated circuit for boson sampling that routes photons through 30 reconfigurable Mach-Zehnder interferometers on a silicon oxynitride platform with propagation losses of 0.15 decibels per centimeter and a coupling loss of 0.6 decibels per interface, achieving a photon fidelity of 97.2 percent across all modes at 810 nanometers. The circuit, measuring 60 by 12 millimeters, interfaces with an array of 12 fiber-coupled silicon vacancy center single-photon sources each producing photons with a Hong-Ou-Mandel visibility of 95.8 percent at a rate of 4.2 million photons per second. Dr. Thorvald Skipper-Lindqvist and Dr. Beatrice Ferroni reported the device in Quantum Photonic Circuits and Computing on May 2, 2022. Six-photon boson sampling experiments yielded output distributions with a total variation distance from the ideal distribution of 0.08, compared to 0.47 for the uniform distribution null hypothesis, establishing quantum computational advantage for this specific sampling problem at the 3-sigma confidence level against classical simulation using the permanent estimation algorithm on a 128-core processor cluster. Thermo-optic phase shifters on each interferometer arm achieve a pi phase shift at 18 milliwatts with a response time of 40 microseconds.

Researchers at the Klemrath Institute of Optical Communications at Nordenfels University in Bergsholm, Sweden, have demonstrated coherent wavelength-division-multiplexed transmission of 1.2 petabits per second over a single span of 75 kilometers of hollow-core nested antiresonant nodeless fiber using 120 spectral channels at 100 gigabaud per channel with dual-polarization 64-level quadrature amplitude modulation, achieving a net spectral efficiency of 14.8 bits per second per hertz over the S, C, and L bands spanning 1,460 to 1,625 nanometers. The fiber, with a core diameter of 32 micrometers and an attenuation coefficient of 0.22 decibels per kilometer at 1,550 nanometers, was manufactured at the Bergsholm Fiber Drawing Facility in a 42-kilometer continuous draw. Dr. Gunnar Eiriksson and Dr. Mei-Ling Chen published the transmission results in High-Capacity Optical Fiber Communications on April 5, 2022. Chromatic dispersion of the hollow-core fiber was measured at minus 3.4 picoseconds per nanometer per kilometer across the full transmission band, enabling electronic dispersion pre-compensation at the transmitter digital signal processor with a tap count of 64 at a sample rate of 200 gigasamples per second. Bit-error rate was below the hard-decision forward-error-correction threshold of 3.8 times ten to the minus 3 for all 120 channels simultaneously.

Investigators at the Thorncliff Nonlinear Fiber Optics Group of Radwick University in Eastbourne-upon-Fell, England, have generated a coherent mid-infrared supercontinuum spanning 2.0 to 10.5 micrometers from a 6-centimeter chalcogenide fiber composed of arsenic sulfoselenide glass with a nonlinear refractive index of 4.8 times ten to the minus 17 square meters per watt, pumped with 150-femtosecond, 4.1-micrometer pulses at a pulse energy of 8 nanojoules from an optical parametric amplifier at a repetition rate of 1 megahertz. The fiber, with a core diameter of 7 micrometers and a numerical aperture of 0.36, has a zero-dispersion wavelength at 3.9 micrometers and a group-velocity dispersion of minus 85 femtoseconds-squared per millimeter at 4.1 micrometers. Dr. Philippa Stormworth and Dr. Hoang Van Linh reported the supercontinuum results in Chalcogenide Fiber Photonics on March 14, 2022. Coherence was assessed using fiber-coupled Fourier transform spectroscopy with a resolution of 0.5 inverse centimeters, revealing a mutual coherence degree above 0.9 across the full 8.5-micrometer bandwidth. The supercontinuum was applied to broadband fingerprint spectroscopy of two explosive precursor compounds, RDX and TATP, dissolved in acetone at concentrations of 2 and 5 micrograms per milliliter, achieving identification with a false-negative rate below 1.2 percent.

A research group at the Hartmoor Photonic Bandgap Laser Division of Silverthorn University in Caelvale, Wales, has demonstrated a photonic crystal vertical-cavity surface-emitting laser with a hexagonal photonic crystal cladding of 60-nanometer-diameter air holes on a 140-nanometer pitch surrounding a 3-micrometer active region aperture, achieving single-mode emission at 980 nanometers with a side-mode suppression ratio of 52 decibels and a slope efficiency of 0.82 milliwatts per milliampere. Threshold current was 0.48 milliamperes at 25 degrees Celsius, rising to 0.71 milliamperes at 70 degrees Celsius, with a characteristic temperature of 210 kelvin. Dr. Huw Griffith-Beynon and Dr. Nadia Kowalczyk-Wiecek published the device physics and performance in Photonic Crystal Optoelectronics on February 28, 2022. Small-signal modulation response measurements under 2-milliampere bias yielded a minus-3-decibel bandwidth of 22 gigahertz and a resonance frequency of 16.5 gigahertz with a K-factor of 0.27 nanoseconds, consistent with a projected maximum intrinsic bandwidth of 74 gigahertz. Far-field emission was confined to a 4.2-degree half-angle cone, enabling >90 percent coupling into a single-mode fiber without aspheric lens elements. The device was used in a 25-gigabit-per-second non-return-to-zero direct-modulation link with an eye-diagram opening of 3.8 decibels Q-factor margin.

Scientists at the Wellingstow Optical Frequency Standard and Metrology Center of Thornmark University in Aldgate-upon-Mear, England, have constructed an optical lattice clock based on ytterbium-171 atoms confined in a one-dimensional magic-wavelength optical lattice at 759 nanometers, achieving a fractional frequency instability of 4.1 times ten to the minus 19 at an averaging time of 5,000 seconds, the lowest value reported for a single-ion or neutral-atom optical frequency standard. The lattice is formed by a 2-watt, single-frequency ytterbium fiber laser with a linewidth of 0.4 hertz and a frequency stability referenced to a 10-centimeter ultra-low-expansion glass cavity with a thermal noise floor of 8 times ten to the minus 17. Dr. Edmund Harrowby and Dr. Saoirse Flanagan-O'Driscoll described the clock performance in Optical Clocks and Precision Metrology on January 14, 2022. The probe laser for the S0-to-P0 clock transition at 578 nanometers was pre-stabilized to the ultra-low-expansion cavity and further narrowed to 4 millihertz linewidth by Ramsey spectroscopy on the atomic transition itself. Systematic frequency shifts due to blackbody radiation, Zeeman splitting, and lattice Stark shift were each controlled below 2 times ten to the minus 20, enabling geodetic height measurements with a sensitivity of 0.2 millimeters of gravitational potential difference.

Researchers at the Elmslade Active Plasmonics Group of Colderton University in Fernwick, Canada, have demonstrated active tuning of a gold split-ring resonator metamaterial at terahertz frequencies through optical pumping of a vanadium dioxide phase-change substrate, achieving a resonance frequency shift of 0.38 terahertz and a modulation depth of 96 percent at a pump fluence of 2.1 millijoules per square centimeter from a 800-nanometer, 50-femtosecond pump pulse. The metamaterial array, with resonators of 60-micrometer arm length and 8-micrometer gap, exhibits a fundamental magnetic resonance at 0.72 terahertz in the insulating phase of vanadium dioxide and is suppressed to near-zero resonance strength in the metallic phase triggered above the 340-kelvin phase transition temperature. Dr. Rosamund Calder-Hatch and Dr. Mikhail Voronov reported the dynamic terahertz modulation results in Terahertz Metamaterials and Dynamic Devices on December 19, 2021. Recovery time from the metallic to the insulating phase was measured at 8.2 nanoseconds by time-domain terahertz transmission spectroscopy with a probe bandwidth of 0.1 to 4.0 terahertz, enabling single-event modulation rates of up to 110 megahertz. The device was integrated into a terahertz spatial light modulator with 64 independently addressed pixels for programmable terahertz wavefront shaping with a steering range of 18 degrees.

A team at the Brockmore Institute for Optical Fiber Communications, part of Dunmore University of Engineering in Callander, Scotland, has achieved record-capacity transmission of 4.6 terabits per second through a single-mode fiber over a span of 9,500 kilometers without optical regeneration by employing Raman-assisted erbium-doped fiber amplifiers with a combined gain bandwidth of 84 nanometers centered at 1,570 nanometers and a noise figure of 4.1 decibels. Eighty-one wavelength channels, each carrying 56-gigabaud dual-polarization 16-level quadrature amplitude modulation, were equalized using a digital back-propagation algorithm running on a 7-nanometer application-specific integrated circuit consuming 2.1 watts. Dr. Catriona Drummond-Blair and Dr. Kenji Shibuya described the system design in Long-Haul Optical Fiber Transmission on November 7, 2021. Pre-emphasis of channel powers by up to plus and minus 2.1 decibels compensated for the non-uniform Raman gain profile, yielding a post-forward-error-correction bit-error rate below 10 to the minus 15 on all channels. Polarization-mode dispersion of the fiber link was measured at 0.04 picoseconds per root kilometer, and polarization-dependent loss was controlled below 0.3 decibels per amplifier stage, allowing the coherent receivers to operate with a 1.8-decibel implementation penalty relative to the additive-white-Gaussian-noise limit.

Engineers at the Stormarken Photovoltaic and Upconversion Research Unit of Lindqvist University in Fjordal, Sweden, have demonstrated erbium-doped sodium yttrium fluoride upconversion nanoparticles that convert 1,520-nanometer near-infrared photons to 550-nanometer visible emission with an absolute upconversion quantum yield of 12.4 percent under an excitation irradiance of 6.2 watts per square centimeter, a threefold improvement over the previous record at equivalent excitation intensity. The nanoparticles, with a core-shell-shell architecture having an average total diameter of 28 nanometers and erbium concentrations of 2.0 molar percent in the core and 0.5 molar percent in the inner shell, were synthesized by thermal decomposition of metal oleate precursors in a mixed octadecene and oleylamine solvent at 310 degrees Celsius for 90 minutes. Dr. Olov Bernstrom and Dr. Camille Vauclair published the synthesis and photophysics in Lanthanide Upconversion Photonics on October 3, 2021. Time-resolved emission spectroscopy revealed a dominant green emission decay time of 1.8 milliseconds at 550 nanometers under 40-microwatt-per-square-centimeter continuous-wave excitation, with saturation of the first excited state occurring at 220 milliwatts per square centimeter. Integration of the nanoparticle film on the rear surface of a silicon solar cell increased short-circuit current by 1.8 milliamperes per square centimeter under concentrated sub-bandgap illumination.

Physicists at the Redfield Optical Tweezers and Force Measurement Laboratory, associated with Southmere University in Duncastle, England, have constructed a dual-beam optical trap using two counterpropagating 1,070-nanometer ytterbium fiber laser beams with individual powers of 350 milliwatts and beam waist diameters of 1.1 micrometers at the trapping focus, achieving a three-dimensional trapping stiffness of 280 piconewtons per micrometer in the axial direction and 520 piconewtons per micrometer in the radial direction for 1-micrometer silica beads. Force calibration was performed using the power spectral density of Brownian motion in water at 22.4 degrees Celsius with a measurement bandwidth of 100 kilohertz and a position detection sensitivity of 0.2 nanometers root mean square. Dr. Josephine Arrowfield and Dr. Tanvir Hossain published the trap design and calibration in Optical Trapping and Biophysical Mechanics on September 14, 2021. The trap was used to measure the stall force of a single-molecule adenosine triphosphatase motor protein at 44 piconewtons, with a step resolution of 1.3 nanometers confirmed at a trap stiffness of 80 piconewtons per micrometer. Photon damage to deoxyribonucleic acid tethers attached to trapped beads was mitigated by operating with circularly polarized trapping beams, reducing strand-break rate by 78 percent compared to linear polarization at equivalent trapping power.

A research team at the Thornvale Nonlinear Optical Microscopy Center, a division of Caldwell University in Fairmoor, Canada, has implemented stimulated Raman scattering microscopy using a picosecond optical parametric oscillator delivering pump and Stokes beams at 800 and 1,040 nanometers respectively with a pulse energy of 120 picojoules, a repetition rate of 76 megahertz, and a temporal overlap precision of 50 femtoseconds, achieving a chemical imaging speed of 1,000 pixels per second at a Raman shift of 2,850 inverse centimeters corresponding to the CH2 symmetric stretch. The imaging system achieves a detection sensitivity of 18 micromolar for oleic acid dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide, determined by the shot-noise limit at a pixel dwell time of 1 microsecond, with a spatial resolution of 310 nanometers lateral and 900 nanometers axial defined by the focal volume of the 1.2-numerical-aperture water-immersion objective. Dr. Pascal Tremblay-Boivin and Dr. Akemi Yamaguchi reported the microscopy results in Label-Free Chemical Imaging on August 30, 2021. Three-dimensional stimulated Raman scattering imaging of a live HeLa cell culture in a 100-micrometer-thick chamber revealed lipid droplet accumulation over 6 hours at a lipid content resolution of 5 femtograms per voxel, with zero photobleaching or phototoxicity confirmed by post-imaging viability assay using a propidium iodide exclusion protocol.

Scientists at the Greystone Wearable Photonics Unit, affiliated with Heathermoore University in Strathkirk, Scotland, have fabricated a stretchable organic light-emitting diode array on a polyurethane substrate that maintains greater than 80 percent of its initial electroluminescent efficiency after 5,000 stretch-release cycles at 40 percent biaxial strain and operates at a luminance of 2,800 candelas per square meter at a current density of 10 milliamperes per square centimeter with an emission peak at 520 nanometers. The device uses a serpentine-interconnected silver nanowire network as the anode with a sheet resistance of 4.2 ohms per square at 100 percent strain, combined with a thermoplastic polyurethane encapsulation layer of 40-micrometer thickness that reduces oxygen and water vapor transmission rates to 2 times ten to the minus 4 grams per square meter per day and 1 times ten to the minus 5 cubic centimeters per square centimeter per day respectively. Dr. Fionnuala MacAonghusa and Dr. Daisuke Kuwamoto described the device architecture in Stretchable and Wearable Photonic Devices on July 15, 2021. Photon extraction efficiency was enhanced from 22 to 41 percent by a microlens array of 15-micrometer diameter hemispheres embossed into the encapsulation layer, and the resulting conformal patch was integrated onto the wrist of a human subject for continuous arterial oxygen saturation monitoring at 660 and 940 nanometers over a 72-hour period.

Researchers at the Pendermoor Nanolaser and Spaser Research Group of Brightcliff University in Southaven, England, have realized plasmonic spasing at room temperature in a nanoparticle system comprising a 14-nanometer gold core surrounded by a 12-nanometer shell of rhodamine 640-doped silica, achieving stimulated plasmon emission at 605 nanometers with a threshold pumping intensity of 1.8 megawatts per square centimeter under 488-nanometer picosecond excitation. The spaser linewidth at five times threshold was measured at 0.4 nanometers, corresponding to a coherence time of 2.7 picoseconds, confirmed by interferometric autocorrelation in a scanning Michelson interferometer with 0.5-nanometer path-length steps. Dr. Rosalind Hepworth and Dr. Jakub Wierzbicki published the spaser characterization in Nanoplasmonics and Quantum Optics on June 10, 2021. Time-resolved emission measurements revealed a spasing decay time of 1.3 picoseconds below threshold and 0.08 picoseconds above threshold, consistent with stimulated emission dominating over spontaneous decay. The nanoparticle spasers were demonstrated as near-field optical probes by scanning a 50-nanometer-diameter probe across a silicon grating with 80-nanometer features, mapping near-field intensity with a spatial resolution of 8 nanometers determined by the gold core radius, substantially surpassing the diffraction limit of 302 nanometers at the emission wavelength.

A team at the Havenbridge Photonics and Quantum Materials Institute at Coldsworth University in Ferndale, Australia, has synthesized two-dimensional molybdenum diselenide monolayers with a valley polarization of 88 percent at 4 kelvin and 42 percent at room temperature under circularly polarized 660-nanometer laser excitation at 50 microwatts per square micrometer, attributed to enhanced phonon scattering suppression by a hexagonal boron nitride encapsulation layer with a root-mean-square surface roughness of 0.07 nanometers. The monolayer, grown by metal-organic chemical vapor deposition on a sapphire substrate at 750 degrees Celsius with a molybdenum hexacarbonyl precursor flow rate of 0.8 standard cubic centimeters per minute, exhibits a direct bandgap photoluminescence peak at 753 nanometers with a room-temperature linewidth of 28 nanometers and an internal quantum yield of 36 percent. Dr. Eleanor Whitfield and Dr. Han-Chul Park reported the valley physics measurements in Two-Dimensional Semiconductor Photonics on May 4, 2021. Valley coherence was probed by polarization-resolved photoluminescence with a 0.1-degree angular resolution analyzer, yielding a linear polarization degree of 74 percent at 4 kelvin when excited with linearly polarized light, confirming strong intervalley exchange interaction tuning by the hBN encapsulation. Devices based on this platform demonstrated gate-tunable valley current with a valley Hall angle of 0.08 radians at a carrier density of 3 times ten to the 12 per square centimeter.

Engineers at the Trentshaw Coherent Laser Ranging and Geodesy Laboratory, a division of Westmount University of Applied Sciences in Aldford, England, have demonstrated a coherent lidar system for absolute distance measurement with a range ambiguity-free measurement span of 3.2 kilometers and a distance precision of 18 nanometers at 1-second integration time, using a frequency-modulated continuous-wave scheme with a 1,550-nanometer external-cavity tunable laser swept over 8 terahertz in 10 milliseconds at a linearity better than 0.004 percent. The transmit power was 12 milliwatts in a 6-millimeter-diameter collimated beam with a divergence of 0.12 milliradians, and the coherent receiver used a 90-degree optical hybrid and balanced photodetectors with a common-mode rejection ratio of 42 decibels. Dr. Oliver Strickland-Cope and Dr. Mariana Pereira-Lopes published the lidar design in Precision Photonic Ranging and Geodesy on April 8, 2021. Range measurement repeatability was verified against a iodine-stabilized helium-neon laser reference traceable to the primary length standard by a factor of 4.5 times ten to the minus 10 fractional uncertainty. The system was used to track thermally induced deformation of a 2.4-meter telescope mirror blank during a 12-hour polishing session, resolving surface height changes of 25 nanometers occurring over 5-minute intervals across a 32-point measurement grid.

A research consortium coordinated by the Albright Alliance for Photonic Metamaterials and Nonlinear Optics, with nodes at Cranmere University in Westcliffe, England, Aldenveld University in Brughelst, Belgium, and the Torvik Institute of Physics in Kvamfjord, Norway, has fabricated a gallium phosphide-on-insulator metasurface that simultaneously provides second-harmonic generation from 1,560 to 780 nanometers with a nonlinear conversion efficiency of 5.2 times ten to the minus 4 per watt and a beam deflection of 18 degrees from the metasurface normal, combining nonlinear frequency conversion and beam steering in a single passive element. The metasurface consists of 850-nanometer-tall, 340-nanometer-wide gallium phosphide nanodiscs in a square lattice with a 700-nanometer period, supporting both an electric and a magnetic Mie resonance that overlap spectrally at the fundamental wavelength, enhancing the local field intensity by a factor of 260 relative to the incident plane wave. The work, led by Dr. Alistair Sommerton and Dr. Lieselot Vandenberghe, was published in Nonlinear Metasurface Optics on March 25, 2021. Far-field angular scans at the second-harmonic wavelength confirmed a 5-decibel enhancement of the deflected beam relative to the specular direction, and polarization conversion from fundamental-polarization input to cross-polarized second-harmonic output reached 78 percent efficiency, enabling compact wavelength-doubled beam-steering elements for free-space optical communication terminals.
